coredata.dc:title: Biomass derived diverse carbon nanostructure for electrocatalysis, energy conversion and storage
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Ying
Jiang, Junjie
Xu, Yaxuan
Wang, Shufei
An, Wei
Chai, Qingshuo
Prova, Umme Hani
Wang, Chunxia
Huang, Guoyong
coredata.dc:description: With the emerging requirement for clean renewable energy and storage system, the advancement of ecofriendly, low-cost, highly active electrode materials has expanded. Biomass, as a natural abundant, renewable source with diverse structure serves as an alternative sustainable source. Recent studies demonstrated that various applications of biomass-derived carbon materials are currently prevailing in electrocatalysis and energy storage and conversion system due to their tunable structure, multiple porosity and rich surface chemistry. Here, in this review, we primarily focus on the principal synthetic strategies of biomass-derived carbon including pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization, chemical vapor deposition, molten salt carbonization, template method along with the assist of microwave and ultrasonication approach. Then, this review strives to highlight the recent development of various dimensions of biomass-derived carbon nanostructure ranging from zero-dimensional carbon dots, one-dimensional carbon nanotubes/fibers/wires, two-dimensional carbon nanosheets to three-dimensional hierarchical carbon in view of pore size and surface area. We also summarize the primary application of biomass-based carbon nanostructure in versatile electrocatalysis, various kinds of secondary batteries, supercapacitors, and other energy related storage fields. Finally, challenges in the development of biomass-based carbon and the large-scale production in industrial fields are addressed.

head.ce:title: Biomass derived diverse carbon nanostructure for electrocatalysis, energy conversion and storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the emerging requirement for clean renewable energy and storage system, the advancement of ecofriendly, low-cost, highly active electrode materials has expanded. Biomass, as a natural abundant, renewable source with diverse structure serves as an alternative sustainable source. Recent studies demonstrated that various applications of biomass-derived carbon materials are currently prevailing in electrocatalysis and energy storage and conversion system due to their tunable structure, multiple porosity and rich surface chemistry. Here, in this review, we primarily focus on the principal synthetic strategies of biomass-derived carbon including pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization, chemical vapor deposition, molten salt carbonization, template method along with the assist of microwave and ultrasonication approach. Then, this review strives to highlight the recent development of various dimensions of biomass-derived carbon nanostructure ranging from zero-dimensional carbon dots, one-dimensional carbon nanotubes/fibers/wires, two-dimensional carbon nanosheets to three-dimensional hierarchical carbon in view of pore size and surface area. We also summarize the primary application of biomass-based carbon nanostructure in versatile electrocatalysis, various kinds of secondary batteries, supercapacitors, and other energy related storage fields. Finally, challenges in the development of biomass-based carbon and the large-scale production in industrial fields are addressed.

Biomass, as a natural abundant source with diverse structure serves as an alternative sustainable source in versatile electrocatalysis, various kinds of secondary batteries, supercapacitors, and other energy related storage fields. The current challenges in the development of biomass-based carbon and the large-scale production in industrial fields are addressed.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The depletion of energy and deterioration of severe environmental pollution arising from the ever-increasing demand of fossil fuels calls for the development of continuous and stable outputs of renewable and sustainable energy storage/conversion technology including electrochemical water splitting, metal-air batteries, secondary battery, supercapacitors and fuel cells [1–4]. The success of these well-developed systems relies on the exploration of cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and high-performance advanced materials. Among the versatile materials, efforts have been made to develop carbon-based nanomaterials as candidates in electrocatalysis and renewable energy storage due to their diverse tunable structure, rich surface chemistry, large surface area as well as excellent chemical stability [5,6]. However, most of these carbonaceous nanomaterials are heavily dependent on fossil products such as coal, pitch or petrochemical products, suffering from harsh synthetic process, energy consumption, low yield, and complex treatment [7], hindering industrial application. Therefore, it is imperative to design easily available, abundant, eco-friendly, scalable, and high-value added carbon nanostructure from low-cost, green, and renewable energy resources [8–10].

As a heart of aforementioned sustainable energy, several important electrocatalysis reaction have been addressed to generate hydrogen energy or produce hydrocarbon fuels [11,12]. Besides, secondary batteries and supercapacitors have attracted much interest for industrial applications in electric vehicle, portable electronic device, and stationary energy system [13,14]. The performance of these energy storage system strongly depends on the advanced materials used in electrochemical system. It is generally acknowledged that porous structure with high specific surface area (SSA) is beneficial for electron and ion transport, which is favorable for electrocatalysis and energy conversion and storage. Moreover, heteroatom could modulate the electronic and crystalline structure of carbon nanomaterials, showing significant influence on the above-mentioned electrochemical performance [15]. Therefore, among the various exploited materials, biomass with rich heteroatoms stood out as a renewable, abundant, sustainable, environmentally-friendly, low cost carbon resource for the preparation of high value-added carbon electrode materials from scientific and practical aspects [16].

Up to now, biomass-based carbon has attracted tremendous interest and sparked intense application due to their environmental friendliness, unique structural characteristics, and high stability. Since 2010, various synthetic strategies have been extensively studied for the fabrication of biomass-derived carbon as depicted in Fig. 1 a. Among them, pyrolysis combined with activation is the most commonly used synthetic method. Moreover, intense research towards the application of biomass-derived carbon for electrocatalysis and energy conversion and storage has flourished. Fig. 1b shows the growing number of papers published in electrocatalysis, batteries, and supercapacitors along with other related fields over the last decades.

As a carbon-rich precursor, biomass with rich heteroatoms stood out as a renewable, sustainable candidate for functional carbon nanomaterials [17,18]. Actually, the annual crop output is about 146 billion tons and the amount of domestic or animal husbandry waste is about 100 billion tons. These biomass are commonly discarded or disposed by direct incineration, causing pollution and pressing environmental problems with low energy utilization [19,20]. Therefore, the transformation of biomass resource into carbon-based materials with desired porous structure and large SSA provides a feasible solution [21]. Most of biomass possess a natural well-ordered structure and contain heteroatoms, which can realize self-doping during the synthetic process, resulting in inherent structure with more defect as active sites [22]. In addition, the heteroatom offers surface functional groups that are beneficial in enhanced conductivity, enabling high electrochemical performance [23].

To date, the conversion of biomass to clean renewable energy system has been proposed in view of versatile applications including electrocatalysis, secondary batteries [24], energy conversion and storage [25]. Initially, researchers pay attention to the synthetic strategies of biomass-derived carbon [26]. Then, the structural diversities of biomass-derived carbon and their application in energy storage have been proposed [27]. For instance, Pan aims at the design of biomass based carbon structures, while Toupance highlights the application of biomass based carbon in supercapacitors and hybrid solar cell applications [28,29]. These review present biomass derived carbon with emphasis on synthetic strategies or refer to part of applications [30]. Therefore, to present a comprehensive and in-depth review in view of synthetic strategies, various dimensions, and applications in electrocatalysis, energy conversion and storage of biomass-derived carbon is still challenging and of vital importance to meet the ever-increasing research of biomass.

In this article, we aimed at recent research progress of biomass-derived carbon nanomaterials including versatile synthetic methods, diverse structural dimensions and their applications in electrocatalysis, energy conversion and storage (Fig. 2 ). We briefly start from the introduction of widely used synthetic methods involving pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization, chemical vapor deposition, molten salt carbonization, template method, as well as the assist of microwave and ultrasonication method. Then, with the above strategies and the choice of activators, the natural biomass with various morphologies can be converted into versatile dimension of biomass-derived carbon ranging from zero-dimensional (0D) carbon dots, one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanotubes/carbon nanofiber, two-dimensional (2D) carbon nanosheets to three-dimensional (3D) hierarchical porous carbon wide range pore size and desired SSA. Additionally, the applications of biomass derived carbon materials were discussed in view of electrocatalysis such as hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), oxygen evolution reaction (OER), oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), carbon dioxide reductions (CO2RR), nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR), secondary batteries including lithium ion batteries (LIBs), lithium sulfur batteries (LSBs), lithium oxygen batteries (LOBs), potassium ion batteries (KIBs), sodium ion batteries (SIBs), supercapacitors, and other energy conversion and storage involving micro fuel cells and microwave absorption, in which we highlight the porous structure, surface area and heteroatom doping effect on the electrochemical performance. Finally, the challenges of biomass derived carbon considering the synthetic approach in industrial production, the underlying mechanism, in situ characterization, the fabrication of biomass in a well desired structure as well as the potential application in related fields are also addressed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic method for biomass derived carbon

ce:section.ce:para: The pyrolysis method usually happens in the tubular furnace under the protection of inert gas like argon (Ar) or nitrogen (N2) and heated for several hours until the complete conversion of biomass into carbonaceous nanomaterials, in which high temperature (500–1000 °C) is essential to control the structure and surface of carbon [31]. During the pyrolysis process, micropores and edge-rich defects can be formed due to the generated water vapor gases and the randomly nucleate of the hydroxyl and amino groups. Meanwhile, the existence of N, P, S elements can achieve self-doping. For instance, Du et al. prepared carrots-based N-doped porous carbon sheets (N-PC) with disordered porous structure through the pyrolysis method (Fig. 3 a) [32]. They found out that increasing temperature could produce more porous structure and higher SSA. Pyrolysis of stems of soybean sprouts under 700 °C under KOH activation will yield N, P, S co-doped carbon [33]. The direct pyrolysis does not require any activation process and the unique structure can be fully kept. As reported, direct pyrolysis of white corncob in the nitrogen atmosphere produce the white corncob-based carbon (CDC) with homogeneous sheet and smooth surface (Fig. 3b) [34]. While further calcination under air atmosphere result in a porous structure. Therefore, the porous structure can be modulated by using different atmospheres during the pyrolysis process.

Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) usually happens within temperature range of 100–250 °C and pressure range of 2–6 MPa by adopting water as the reaction medium [35]. HTC have the advantages of environmentally friendly, low cost, simple operation and high efficiency. However, the HTC derived carbon shows poorly formed porosity and low SSA, therefore, subsequent activation method is needed. HTC process usually yields soluble organic acid and aldehydes and insoluble heteroatom doped nanomaterials with controllable surface and porosity. Hu et al. successfully prepared N self-doped multi-stage porous carbon by using penicillin fermentation residue as precursor under HTC and subsequent KOH and ZnCl2 chemical activation (Fig. 4 a) [36]. The obtained carbon showed loosely 3D coral-like carbon structure with uniform pores.

Moreover, combined HTC with pyrolysis together can realized the synthesis of N, O co-doped petal-like hierarchical porous carbon derived from waste bamboo (Fig. 4b) [37]. The bamboo shaving was hydrothermally pretreated with H3PO4 at 180 °C for 12 h and then pyrolyzed with KOH activation under 700 °C in the presence of N2 atmosphere. Hydrothermal pretreatment under H3PO4 can promote the development of pores while act as a catalytic hydrothermal agent. The KOH activation contributes to the pore creation and pore expansion. Meanwhile, palm kernel shell (PKS) based carbon foam can be prepared by HTC at 250 °C and pyrolyzed at 600 °C to yield a porous foam structure [38]. Specifically, PKS-lignin powder was softened from 146.5 to 210 °C. With the increasing pyrolysis temperature from 250 °C to 600 °C, gasses (e.g., CO, H2, CO2, CH4, H2O and volatile compounds) derived from the oxygen-containing functional groups were released, leading to the formation of bubbles.

As for chili straw derived porous carbon, the increase of carbonization temperature induced a richer pore structure with large SSA, which could provide more active sites and facilitate ion transport [39]. Upon increasing the pyrolysis temperature, the SSA of porous carbon increased from 1921.31 m2 g−1 to 2768.52 m2 g−1, and the total pore volume increased from 1.25 cm3 g−1 to 1.40 cm3 g−1. When temperature reached 1700 °C, alkali lignin-based carbon with well-organized graphene 2D sheets with interconnected bowl-like structure can be fabricated [40]. As the pyrolysis temperature raise up to 1700 °C, Na2CO3and NaCl salts decomposed to sodium metal and evaporated, favored the formation of a porous carbon structure.

As mentioned above, we found out that temperature is considered as the predominant parameter affecting surface area and porosity. When the pyrolysis temperature is under 400 °C, complete devolatilization of volatile constituents cannot be realized, and consequently brings about the blockage of some pores and hinders the formation of new pores. The number of pores (especially micropores) and the SSA of biomass will increase. When the pyrolysis temperature exceeds 450 °C, hierarchically macro-/meso-/microporous structure can be produced. A dramatically increase of surface area is observed at 400–500 °C, this increase trend may continue until 900 °C. As the temperature goes up, amorphous carbon is transformed into crystalline carbon via condensation, more volatiles are removed, sparse regions are created, cracks and more pores are generated. Further increase of temperature above 900 °C might lead to collapse of micropores formed in the early stage or the combination of micropores into mesopores and macropores. To conclude, 400–700 °C is the proper pyrolysis temperature while the optimum temperature varies by the nature of biomass.

Activation method. The pore structure of the pyrolyzed carbon is not fully developed, hence, the subsequent physical and chemical activation is employed. The physical activation can be roughly divided into steam and carbon dioxide (CO2) activation [41]. While chemical activation can be roughly classified into acid, alkali and salt activation [42]. During the physical activation, the oxidizing atmosphere reacts with biomass carbon to generate carbon oxides, offering micropores and mesopores in the biomass based carbon [43]. Steam activation is the most representative activation process in which water vapor would be absorbed on the surface of carbon to protect oxygen to react with carbon monoxide to form carbon dioxide [44]. However, the conductivity of the steam activated product is relatively low due to the abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface [45]. Pine sawdust derived activated carbon with large porosity and enhanced mesopore can be obtained through steam activation [46]. CO2 activation features with relatively slow reaction rate, which is easy to control the activation process, but it requires a high temperature of 850–1100 °C [47]. At high temperature, CO2 reacts with carbon in the precursor to form pores in the carbon skeleton. Amazonian andiroba shell-based carbon with smooth surface and abundant pores via HTC and CO2 activation process can be prepared (Fig. 5 a) [48]. Moreover, after CO2 activation, the porosity and surface area of hemp fibers based carbon increased (Fig. 5b) [49].

Differ from physical activation, the chemical activation has the merits of short reaction time, controllable activation reaction with the use of chemical activators involving acids (H3PO4, H2SO4), alkaline (KOH, NaOH), and salts (ZnCl2, Na2CO3) at 300–950 °C [50]. Treating pinecones with different chemical activators could yield different morphology of porous carbon (PC) (Fig. 6 a) [51]. The presence of KOH and ZnCl2 produces micropore which is different from that of NaOH and K2CO3 activated porous carbon. Among them, the sample activated by the K2CO3 has the ordered, dense, and honeycomb-like porous structure. Acid activation is widely used to transfer plant fiber like forestry biomass to carbon in the past few decades [52]. The phosphoric acid (H3PO4) activation featuring with low cost and higher yield has been widely applied in industry production. Eucalyptus chips-based carbon material (NAC) with well-informed pore structure and high porosity was prepared with H3PO4 activation at 500 °C (Fig. 6b) [53]. H3PO4-assisted activation combined with carbonization even fabricated N, P, S co-doped walnut shell derived hierarchical porous carbon (WSPC) (Fig. 6c) [54]. The H3PO4 activated WSPC had a comparatively balanced micro- and meso-porous structure with high SSA and pore volume.

Due to the low reaction temperature, high conversion rate and well-developed micropores, alkali activation is broadly applied to activate carbon materials, in which KOH is the representative alkali activator [55]. KOH would react with the carbon to generate K2CO3 and decompose to generate K2O and CO2 to form porous structure. Meanwhile, the K2O would react with K2CO3 and C to form metal K which would diffuse into carbon layer to activate carbon. For instance, rice husk (RH) based porous carbon with high specific area can be prepared by pyrolysis with KOH activation (Fig. 7 a) [56]. After KOH activation, many new macropores appeared and orderly graphitic structures can be prepared. For instance, Walnut shell based microporous carbon through carbonization with KOH activation was also suggested by Serafin's team [57].

In addition, salt activation could effectively tune the pore structure, pore size and SSA. Among the various salt activators, ZnCl2, one of the most representative salt activators, which acts as both dehydrating agent and deoxygenation agent during the carbothermal reduction [58]. Hatice's group use ZnCl2 as activator and suggest coconut shell-based carbon materials with porous and granular structure [59]. ZnCl2 enabled the formation of granular surface pores which contribute to the high SSA. Moreover, coconut shell-based graphene-like carbon nanosheets with 3D porous structure was synthesized under K2CO3 activation (Fig. 7b) [60].

Self-activation can form porous structure without any activators, in which the emitted gas can be used for activation when the biomass is carbonized [61]. Self-activation can avoid the use of toxic and corrosive activation chemicals. Chen et al. take full advantage of the release of CO2 from pyrolysis reaction which promoted the self-activation of carbonized wood during the carbonization process to prepare Zelkova serrata (Thunb) Makino wood derived porous carbon (Fig. 7c). The product treated under 500 °C exhibited a dense surface with regular edges and a number of micro- and meso-pores. With the increase of temperature, the surface turned uneven and edges became irregular until completely etched [62].

Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is considered as one of the most prominent and well-developed techniques [63]. The CVD method involves the deposition of final product on a hot substrate in the vapor phase by using thermal heating, radiation, or plasma usually occurring at a gradient thermal heating [64]. Thirumal et al. turn tamarind fruit shells (TFSs) into porous 3D carbon nanosheets via CVD and the subsequent activation (Fig. 8 a) [65]. The TFSs was pyrolyzed at 800 °C to produce graphitic carbon nanosheets and the subsequent treatment with KOH to produce porous structure. Treating tea-waste with CVD method by adding phosphate as an activator could yield H3PO4-treated carbon (AC-HP) with defect-free morphology. The direct thermal conversion by CVD also generate a high-purity carbon nanofibers (CNFs) network catalyzed by carbon black from corn stalk (Fig. 8b) [66]. The rough surface of carbon black can catalyze the growth of CNFs during CVD, resulting in a fibrous structure with superior stability.

Two-step CVD technology typically refers to the thermal conversion of biomass to steam and steam to carbon nanomaterials, generally occurring in two isolated reactors. For instance, a two-step pyrolysis gasification of wood chips is reported, where the gas generated from the first step is catalyzed to generate carbon [67]. Through decomposition of carbon precursor mixed with nitrogen containing gas on the surface of metal catalyst, solid material is deposited from the gas phase to give graphene-like N doped carbon material [68].

Microwave-assisted chemical vapor deposition (MCVD) method is conducted at low temperature. Microwaves contribute to the expansion of micropores and enhanced SSA [69]. Based on MCVD, a tubular and multi-wall carbon nanotubes (CNTs) from pine nut shell (PNS) chars can be achieved [70]. Due to the uniform, rapid, and batch heating of the microwave, the length, density, and the yiled of biomass based carbon increased.

Molten salt carbonization (MSC) method thermochemically converts biomass to carbon by employing salt or muti-salt as the molten medium [71]. It can be considered as a complementary to HTC approach, which has a higher degree of carbonization. Mixture of KCl and KOH calcined in molten salt can be used as protective medium and structure guide to produce hierarchical porous carbons (HPCs) from wood chips (Fig. 9 a) [72]. The pore size distribution of carbon microstructure was regulated by the content of KOH and the addition of heteroatoms. In addition, the mixture of molten salts can serve as templates, in which KHCO3 is a suitable pore forming agent that can straightforwardly pyrolyze biomass to N, O co-doped 3D hierarchical porous structure [73].

Moreover, eutectic LiCl–KCl is able to prepare porous carbon from wood sawdust (Fig. 9b) [74]. The inert KCl salt acts as thermal medium, which protects the burning of carbon products, and forms an open pore structure [75]. Agaric based carbon nanomaterials with ultra-thin porous carbon nanosheets were also developed by using LiCl–KCl solution as a molten salt (MS) system [76]. The MSC method allows the precipitation of salt from the molten phase and their embedding in carbon, which acts as a template to produce multiple pores. Besides, abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups was produced with the engagement of molten salts. Among these investigations, eutectic Na2CO3–K2CO3 possesses excellent characteristics for the carbonization of various biomass [77]. Zeng et al. revealed that the syngas yield of eutectic Li2CO3–Na2CO3–K2CO3 was significantly higher at 750–850 °C compared to the rapid pyrolysis of biomass [78]. Besides, bamboo shells derived carbon can be prepared at 850 °C with Na2CO3–K2CO3 [79].

Template carbonization accurately tunes the pore structure of carbon-based materials at nanoscale by adding template agents, which significantly increase the SSA and enable a well-organized porous structure [80]. The hard template method employs a chemical or electrochemical reaction to load precursor monomers into template, and the porous material can be obtained after the removal of template [81]. Hard templates such as zeolites, SiO2 and metal oxides with strong rigidity can be used to achieve ordered structure with uniform micro- and meso-pore size. For instance, Hu et al. synthesized lotus seed shell based porous carbon with sodium phytate as a template precursor and phosphorus source (Fig. 10 a) [82]. The soluble sodium phytate is converted pyrolytically to Na5P3O10 nanoparticles in the carbonization process, which are further converted to Na2CO3 and Na3PO4 nanoparticles by reaction with NaOH, and then encapsulated in the carbon matrix as in situ hard template, leaving large mesopores/macropores after template removal. Hierarchical porous carbon with high SSA (820 m2 g−1) can be successfully prepared via a ZnO-based hard template method from rubber-wood sawdust [83].

As a hard template, calcium carbonate (CaCO3) can facilitate the process of activation and control the pore structure. Sevilla et al. prepared porous graphite carbon nanosheets (PGCSs) using bio-derived substances like glucose, glucosamine, soybean powder and microalgae [84]. The resulted nanosized calcium oxide nanoparticles were washed using hydrochloric acid, eventually forming the macroporous foam-like structures. Li's group also employed CaCO3 as template and K2C2O4 as activator to synthesize multi-stage porous carbon derived from corn stover (Fig. 10b) [85]. In addition, Gu et al. employed a calcium acetate (CA) template method to construct porous carbon derived from cotton/polyester textile waste (PC-CA). During this process, CA could break the chemical bond of raw material and decompose to CaCO3, which contribute to the formation of mesoporous structure as in situ hard template and subsequently decomposed to CaO to produce mesopores (Fig. 10c) [86].

Soft template method usually involves well-ordered aggregates formed by amphiphilic molecules such as block copolymers and surfactants, when the concentration surpasses the critical micelle concentration, they will self-assemble into micelles or vesicles [87]. Soft template can be straightforwardly removed in the pyrolysis process, however, the porosity decreased during the depolymerization procedure. Tu et al. prepared a catalyst-CNTs from lignin by soft template method (Fig. 11 a) [88]. The lignin@catalyst micelle system efficiently tunes the size and distribution of surface metal nanoparticles on the silicon wafers. Soft templates like F127, P123 or CTAB are frequently employed to realize an ordered structure [89]. For instance, hydrothermal mesoporous batatas biochar (HMC) was suggested via employing F127 as soft-template (Fig. 11b) [90]. F127 contribute to higher SSA and smooth surface, result in an interconnected and perfectly formed carbon spheres with comparatively homogeneous size. In addition, Ni-doped ordered mesoporous carbon (Ni@OMC) was also reported by taking F127 as soft template, which displayed a fully-developed, orderly mesoporous structure where Ni particles were homogeneously distributed over the entire carbon framework [91].

Considering that hard templates produce larger pore volumes but comparatively destabilized mesopores, while soft templates offer relatively stable mesopores with lower total pore volume and surface area, a dual template approach was brought in to curb their limitation [92]. Lu et al. synthesized FeOx embedded Fe–N doped carbon (Hemin/NPC) via carbonization by employing acorn shell and Mg5(OH)2(CO3)4 and NaCl as dual templates [93]. The removal of template by acid washing generates abundant mesopores, while the melt NaCl nano-droplets was immersed into carbon framework to produce porous structure (Fig. 12 a). By substituting with ZnCl2, waste tea leaf derived layered porous carbon with surface area up to 1968.6 m2 g−1 was expected. Furthermore, Zhou et al. designed and synthesized spirulina derived nitrogen-doped carbon material possessing high SSA and multi-level pore structure by using SiO2 and zinc nitrate as template. When SiO2 was applied as template, the obtained mesoporous carbon, a fluffy and porous accumulated carbon structure can be obtained. In addition, owing to the generation and vaporization of zinc metal, zinc nitrate could generate pores under high temperature [94].

The template method demands stringent operating conditions and steady pre-treatment, thereby hinders the practical application. Self-template refer to the sacrifice of template during the formation of desired mesopores in macroporous hollow structure [95]. For example, Sun's group constructed N, O co-doped self-template corncob based porous carbon via carbonization and KOH activation (Fig. 12b) [96]. With the assistance of self-template, high SSA ranging from 2000 m2 g−1 to 3000 m2 g−1 can be easily generated. Huang et al. fabricated multi-heteroatom (O, N, P and S) self-doped porous carbon with intrinsic metal as self-template of Chinese medicine dregs (MDs) [97]. After KOH activation, maximum SSA reaches up to 2871 m2 g−1 and mesopore ratio of 47.3% with an appropriate heteroatom doping is expected. These results demonstrate that self-sacrificing template not only provides larger pores to decrease ion diffusion resistance, but also introduces heteroatoms into carbon framework to enhance charge mobility.

Electrospinning is one of the most effective techniques to prepare tubular and fibrous nanomaterials with the diameter ranging from few nanometers to micrometers under the spinning voltage range of 3–25 kV [98]. Currently, electrospinning and the integration with CVD or HTC can tailor structural and compositional features [99,100]. Chen et al. proposed electrostatic spinning to achieve CNFs production without fractionation via directly carbonizing nanofiber mats with sugarcane bagasse (SCB), followed by homogeneous esterification with various acid anhydrides [101]. Niu et al. prepared lignin derived nanofiber films through electrospinning process (Fig. 13 a) [102]. The adoption of PVA as a thickening agent in the spinning system enhanced spinnability. Meanwhile, co-axial electrospinning is also applied to produce Jussara pulp-based carbon materials, in which CNFs prepared by coaxial electrostatic spinning had more complete structure, more uniform size and better thermal stability [103].

Ultrasonic method features with a high energy density to produce expansion and compression, creating negative pressure across the liquid medium to reduce clustering [104]. It can be completed within a short time, low temperature and high output, however, the size of carbon is hard to control [105]. Thus, ultrasonic treatment is often used as an assistant method for pyrolysis. Chen et al. proposed a convenient approach to prepare soybean residue-based channel-rich carbon materials by using ultrasound-assisted etching and KOH treatment [106]. Ultrasonic-assisted methods were demonstrated to allow deep etching of carbon materials, thereby inducing more porous structures. The existence of extra pore structures provides additional active sites, facilitates the electrolyte transfer, and optimizes the electrochemical performance.

Microwave assisted activation cause molecules to oscillate and move at high speeds, generates large amounts of heat [107,108]. Microwave irradiation activation of biomass-derived pyrolytic carbon is primarily attributed to CO, CO2, H2 and H2O gases released during the carbonization process (Fig. 13b) [109]. Compared to the traditional heating devices, it can heat at molecular level, raise the temperature quickly, cause fewer defects and enhance the mass transfer [110,111]. Zhang et al. used corn straw as raw material to obtain activated carbon with rich functional groups via microwave-assisted pyrolysis [112].

The fabrication of porous carbon from a wide range of biomass by diverse synthetic method offers fascinating characteristics, which can be used in renewable energy system. As presented in Table 1 , we summarize the most commonly used synthetic methods for biomass derived various dimensioanl carbon. Pyrolysis method usually carries out in the tubular furnace under 500–1000 °C with assist of inert gas. The release of the gases and decomposition of the organic compounds during the pyrolysis process result in the formation of pores, especially micropores (internal diameters less than 2 nm) with SSA in the range of 10–300 m2 g−1 and thus contribute to limited porosity. However, the pyrolysis produces high heat loss and requires long reaction time which is not cost effective. HTC proceeds under 100–250 °C in a closed system, displaying improved energy consumption, minimized toxic wastes generation, higher yield, rich surface hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups. However, the HTC treated carbon exhibits lower surface area, smaller pore volume and inhomogeneous porosity. Therefore, physical activation and chemical activation is necessary to produce well-developed and organized micropore structure with large SSA, however, the subsequent washing process complex the synthetic procedure. CVD offers high purity and mechanical strength of carbon while high temperature will definitely cause the mass loss and shrinkage. MSC method with higher heat capacity can provide a wider operating temperature window and prevent carbon shrinkage. Template carbonization can obtain porous carbon materials with a high SSA, narrow pore size distribution, and highly ordered pore structure, which is crucial for improving the ion and mass transport. However, the removal of template is complex and in most cases is harmful to environment.

Biomass-based 0D carbon nanostructure have aroused tremendous interests because of their good chemical stability, rich surface chemistry and easy modification [120]. Baragau et al. used glucose to produce carbon quantum nanodots (CQDs) by a continuous hydrothermal flow synthesis under supercritical water. The obtained CQDs were irregularly round with a particle size distribution ranging from 1 to 9 nm [121]. Zhang et al. developed a novel polyethyleneimine (PEI) functionalized carbon dot s (CDs) derived from broccoli stems through hydrothermal method (Fig. 14 a) [122]. The obtained CDs showed a spherical shape with an average size of 2.20 nm and a lattice space of 0.21 nm, demonstrating that the CDs may possess graphite-like crystal structure. Synthesis of N,O functionalized CDs from Poa Pratensis was also reported by Krishnaiah's group (Fig. 14b) [123]. The well distributed CDs showed a round shape with an average size of 9 nm and a relatively narrow size distribution.

Biomass precursors have been used to synthesize carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which consists graphitic tubular structures with diameters within nanometer and lengths up to a few millimeters [124,125]. For instance, Liu et al. prepared bamboo derived bamboo-like carbon nanotubes (BCNTs) with multiple intervals and diameter ranging from 50 nm to 300 nm [126]. Large scale production of N-doped CNTs derived from the biomass of typha orientalis through carbonization and molten salt method has also been fabricated [127]. The diameter of the CNTs is about 150 nm with SSA of 281.3 m2 g−1 and the total pore volume of 0.22 cm3 g−1. Cao et al. fabricated kapok derived N-self doped carbon microtubes (HCMT) with hierarchical porous hollow structure under low temperature pre-carbonization in conjunction with KOH activation (Fig. 15 a) [128]. The obtained HCMT shows a high SSA (1600 m2 g−1) and pore volume (0.90 m3 g−1). The unique hollow structure acts as a channel for electron and ion transfer. Moreover, the abundant slits on the surface are favorable for sufficient charge storage and high Coulombic efficiency, the distorted lattice stripes adjacent to the HCMT's edge belonging to the disordered carbon structure favors high electron conductivity.

Carbon nanofibers (CNFs) refer to the fibrous nano-carbon material with diameter of 10–500 nm and the length distribution of 0.5–100 μm. The fiber structure features with good suppleness, high strength and thermal stability [129]. Varieties of bio-sustainable resources especially cellulose and lignin are the most promising precursors employed in the production of CNFs because of its good flexibility, high carbon content, excellent thermal stability along with abundant benzene ring structures [130,131]. For instance, Zhu et al. suggested lignin-based CNFs by a modification strategy combined with electrospinning method [132]. The obtained CNFs possessed a separate filamentary morphology and a smooth surface with a minimum fiber diameter of 260 nm. Meanwhile, Zhong et al. successfully fabricate the nano fibrillated cellulose-based CNFs with the diameter range of 50–250 nm through molten-salt method combined with carbonization process [133]. Unfortunately, it is hard to develop biomass-based CNFs via direct carbonization since cellulose is less thermally stable, and the non-linear molecular structure and inflexibility of lignin lead to independent spinning. Thus, Yin et al. synthesized self-supported ultra-microporous lignin-based CNFs through an electrostatic spinning approach, prior to the air stabilization and argon carbonization process (Fig. 15b) [134]. The pore structure and SSA can be controlled by changing the ratio of lignin mass to polyacrylonitrile. Cao et al. also fabricated lignin-cellulose based CNFs with surface area up to 837.404 m2 g−1 by a short pre-oxidation and carbonization treatment process [135]. Besides, lemongrass derived CNFs with hierarchical porous structure was proposed via an integrated chemical activation and combustion method [136]. Chitin derived nitrogen self-doped carbon nanofibers (CChCNs) by hydrolysis and carbonization process, and lignin derived B, N, F tri-doped carbon nanofibers (BNF-LCFs) by electrospinning and pyrolysis method both possessed a cross linked fiber structure with abundant porosity, providing a shorten electron/ion transport [137,138].

Biomass-derived carbon nanowires (CNWs) are highly biocompatible [139] with high surface-to-volume ratio, good mechanical properties, excellent size/surface-dependent electronic properties together with electrical conductivity [140]. Moreover, an improved capacitive properties can be obtained compared to the native nanowires [141].

Biomass derived 2D carbon with porous layered structure, heteroatom doping, and carbon defects can result in high SSA, more active sites, good conductivity, and enhanced specific capacitance [142,143]. For instance, Wang et al. impregnated pomelo peel and then carbonized to synthesize heteroatom doped porous layer stacked carbon microsheets (Fig. 16 a) [144]. The as prepared N, P doped 2D carbon retains the microstructure of precursor and presents a relatively loose sheets with uniform thickness of 0.3 μm. Guan et al. also fabricated pine nut shell based N doped carbon sheets with appropriate pore size distribution via KOH activation [145].

Besides, biomass derived 2D graphene-like carbon had also been developed [146]. Zhao et al. prepared pomelo peels based graphene like porous carbon nanosheets (GPCN) via hydrothermal process with HAc and H2O2 (Fig. 16b) [147]. The thickness and porosity can be tuned by changing the content of HAc and H2O2. Defect rich, N-doped graphene like nanocarbon sheets (GPNCS) was also constructed by hydrothermal and activation approach by employing fruits of glossy privet as precursor (Fig. 16c) [148]. The obtained material exhibited high SSA, and the presence of N dopants and defects greatly enhance the electrochemical performance.

3D porous structure essentially reduces ion transport resistance and diffusion distance, thereby improve ion-electrode interactions, result in good electrical conductivity. Most multi-dimensional biomass can be converted directly into 3D electrode materials [149]. Fu et al. synthesized 3D hierarchical porous carbon derived from walnut shell under KOH activation process (Fig. 17 a) [150]. Owing to large SSA (1037.31 m2 g−1) and micropore volume (0.51 cm3 g−1), faster ion diffusion and lower charge transfer resistance was expected. Coconut shell derived activated carbon-sulfur (ACS) composite can be successfully prepared by solvothermal method (Fig. 17b) [151]. The ACS reveals a large SSA of 1864 m2 g−1 and a hierarchical porosity spanning from micropore, mesopore to macropore. This structure makes the sulfur particles fully fill the activated carbon pores of ACS composite, when applied as cathode materials in LSBs, the discharge capacity and cycle stability was enhanced.

The proper doping of N and high graphitic N content of biomass enable a superior long-term stability. Taro stem precursor yield a 3D N-doped porous carbon with SSA of 1012 m2 g−1 and high N content of 4.8% (Fig. 17c) [152]. Tan et al. prepared high graphitic porous biomass carbon (HGPBC) from dandelion by one-step activation-carbonization with K2FeO4 [153]. The interconnected flake-like layered 3D structures can enhance ion transport ability. Besides, the presence of microporous, mesoporous, and macroporous coexisting structures can promote good electron transfer , leading to higher power output [154].

The porous activated carbon can be prepared from animal waste by carbonization and KOH activation [155]. Chen et al. obtained turtle shell-based carbon (TSHC-5) by using inorganic salt as salt template and chemical activator. TSHC-5 shows a well-established honeycomb structure with high SSA of 2828 m2 g−1 and pore volume of 1.91 cm3 g−1 [156]. The 3D porous structure and the heteroatom doping enable fast ion exchange in electrochemical reaction process. Chicken red blood cells were also used for the construction of 3D graphene like macro-porous hybrids (GO-RBCs-F-P) [157]. The interlayer length of GO-RBCs-F-P was 0.35 nm and its pore distribution was about 7–22 nm. The hybrid exhibited continuous carbon networks and interconnected macro- and meso-pores, which provide fast electron and mass transport [158]. Besides, by employing chicken foot-based collagen as precursor, sponge shaped carbon (Fig. 18 a) with 3D porous structure was constructed [159]. The distinctive porous structure of graphene-integrated porous carbon possessed large surface area of 1087 m2 g−1 with the average pore size of 4.2 nm.

3D solid networks of carbon such as aerogels with mesoporous structure can preserve the overall performance of carbon. For instance, Xia et al. constructed a flexible electrode with outstanding mechanical properties using wood-derived carbon fiber-reinforced cellulose nanofiber/multi-walled carbon nanotube hybrid aerogels (CF–CNF/MWCNT-HAs) (Fig. 18b) [160]. Cellulose nanofibers with a network structure consisting of a large number of long nanofibers and mutual crosslinking structures, are ideal precursors to form 3D nanostructure. Nitrogen-doped carbon aerogels (C-NGD) derived from a calcined mixture of glucose, dicyandiamide, and nanofibers were also developed (Fig. 18c) [161]. The 3D mesoporous structure with unique conductive network and hollow nanofiber structure improves ion accessibility while retains high electrical conductivity.

In summary, biomass derived carbon nanostructure can be optimized to yield abundant porosity, high SSA, good electrical conductivity and rich surface chemistry. The biomass based 0D carbon features with ultra-small size with rich surface functional groups have been widely designed as battery storage materials to improve the cycling stability of the electrode. 1D carbon have high aspect ratios and high anisotropy, which makes it easier to be assembled into a binder-free and interconnected network to form a strong film in flexible energy storage devices. The 1D linear channel can provide hollow tubular structure and direct current path and facilitate electron conduction and ion transport. As a conductive carbon matrix, 2D biomass-based carbon nanosheets endow themselves with large SSA and more voids to create more active sites and generous storage space for ions. The electrolyte solution can react with the active material quickly through voids, resulting in rapid reaction kinetics. Meanwhile, the electrons are effectively transmitted along carbon sheets, which greatly improves the conductivity. Furthermore, 3D porous carbon owns more open transport channels with well-interconnected pores not only provides a continuous electron pathway for ion accessibility, but also contributes to the acceleration of ion transfer by shortening the diffusion pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in electrocatalysis and energy conversion and storage

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical energy conversion technologies comprising the conversion of electrical energy to clean energy and vice versa is able to meet the needs of renewable energy [162]. Hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are two half-cell reaction of water splitting to produce H2 and O2, and production of hydrogen peroxide by oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is essential to rechargeable metal-air batteries [163]. Moreover, CO2 electrocatalytic reduction reaction (CO2RR) could convert CO2 into methane, formic acid and carbon monoxide are now emerging [164]. Nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR) generates the various products such as ammonia (NH3), hydrazine (N2H4), and/or diazene (N2H2), which are necessary for industry and agriculture [165]. Biomass rich in heteroatoms can achieve self-doping, create more active sites and natural channel structures, which may result in high SSA and accelerate ion transfer in electrocatalysis. The fabrication of highly efficient electrocatalyst derived from biomass have attracted world-wide attention in recent years [166–168].

HER is cathode reaction of the water splitting. Developing high activity and stable electrocatalyst is vital importance to improve the efficiency of HER [169]. Noble metal shows high activity and efficiency towards HER, however the high cost greatly hinders their large-scale industrial production. Carbon-based metal free electrocatalyst including amorphous carbon black, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and mesoporous graphite had attracted much interests as a promising alternative to the precious metal electrocatalysts [170]. Recently, biomass-based carbon had come into the stage owing to its low cost, abundance, rich heteroatoms and unique porous structure which can promote the ion transfer and conductivity.

Sankar et al. prepared biomass rice husks derived corrugated graphene nanosheets (RH-CG) with high HER activity by a carbonization and KOH activation process (Fig. 19 a) [171]. The obtained RH-CG-700 with interlinked graphene nanosheets displays a large SSA of 1154 m2 g−1, rich porosity, and high conductivity. This distinctive structure effectively promotes electron/ion transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface, resulting in a low overpotential (9 mV at 10 mA cm−2) together with small Tafel slope (31 mV dec−1). Since biomass is rich in heteroatoms, self-doped biomass-based carbon would offer more active sites and improved conductivity compared to the pristine materials [172]. For instance, Saravanan et al. prepared peanut shells derived nitrogen-self doped activated carbon material (PSAC) with multi-layered or shortly stacked nanosheet-like structure via pyrolysis combined with activation method (Fig. 19b) [173]. The optimized PSAC possesses high SSA (2338.56 m2 g−1) and uniform mesoporous structure, resulting in more active sites to enhance the electrochemical activity, therefore delivering a Tafel slope of 75.7 mV dec−1 which is comparable to transition metal-doped carbon and superior to graphene structures.

Meanwhile, multi-heteroatom doping give rise to an increased charge density. For instance, the activity and the confined diffusion current density of N, S co-doped mesoporous carbon are about 2.5 and 1.5 times higher than those of N-doped carbon alone [174]. Atchudan et al. fabricated lotus seedpod (LP) and lotus stem (LS) based S, N co-doped porous carbon (LP-PC and LS-PC) with irregular morphology of carbon granules by one-step pyrolysis (Fig. 19c) [175]. Both of the LP-PC and LS-PC showed less defects, higher graphitization and rich N, S functional groups. The LP-PC (457 m2 g−1) and LS-PC (313 m2 g−1) show an optimized HER with the lowest Tafel slope values of 69 and 85 mV dec−1, respectively.

Moreover, N, P and Ca self-doped biochar (PBC) from animal bones via a simple pyrolysis treatment was suggested by Deng et al. [176]. Due to desired specific area (279.6 m2 g−1), low charge transfer resistance and good conductivity (0.053 ± 0.004 S cm−1), the excellent HER activity of PBC-800 is equivalent or much better than the synthesized carbon-based electrocatalysts with single heteroatom doping. It shows both low onset overpotential (80 ± 3 mV) and overpotential (162 ± 3 mV) under current density of 10 mA cm−2, possessing a small Tafel slope of 80 mV dec−1 and a large exchange current density of 52.5 μA cm−2 (Fig. 19d).

OER is 4-electron transfer process (4OH− → 2H2O + 4e− + O2) which is sensitive to pH. However, the process is kinetically slow and has a high overpotential (1.23 V). Nowadays, iridium/ruthenium (Ir/Ru), and their oxides, alloys, and composites had been considered as the most appropriate OER catalysts because they can lower the activation energy threshold. However, these materials suffers from high cost, low stability, which restricted their practical application [177]. Thus, the fabrication of low-cost, high performance and environmentally friendly OER catalysts is highly desirable [178,179]. Biomass with nitrogen content, disordered structure could enhance conductivity, tune electron distribution, and offer rich defect sites to optimize the properties of the carbon material [180].

For instance, interconnected hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets (HPCNS) based on cedar tree cone were synthesized via one-step pyrolysis process for OER (Fig. 20 a) [115]. The obtained HPCNS possess a tight, amorphous, 3D homogeneous porous structure which can achieve fast ion transport, resulting in improved electrocatalytic activity due to highest surface areas (2954 m2 g−1), conductivities (11.03 S cm−1) and large pore volumes (1.35 cm3 g−1). Meanwhile, the existence of N is able to regulate the number and distribution of active sites on HPCNS. HPCNS-700 exhibited outstanding OER performance with low overpotential (106 mV) under 10 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH solution and low Tafel slope (191 mV dec−1). Liu et al. prepared corn stalks-based N-doped porous carbon nanosheets (NPCSS) with few-layered structure by pyrolysis (Fig. 20b) [181]. The obtained NPCSS had pore size of 3.77 nm and BET surface areas of 317.5 m2 g−1 along with enormous active sites. Therefore, a high activity of OER with overpotentials of 358 mV under 10 mA cm−2, lower Tafel slope of 144 mV dec−1, and negligible degradation after 1000 cycles can be obtained, comparing to non-N doped material (PCSS of 239 mV dec−1). Wu et al. synthesized uniformly N, P, Fe co-doped mesoporous carbon microspheres (egg-CMS) based on eggs via a self-doping approach [182]. The egg-CMS exhibited high SSA (970 m2 g−1) and pore volume (0.5 cm3 g−1) with low onset potentials (1.51 V) and Tafel slope (59 mV dec−1), large current densities (74.6 mA cm−2 at a potential of 1.6 V), and superior stabilities for OER.

ORR involves a multi-electron transfer process. During the reaction, O2 can be either directly electrochemically reduced to water (direct four-electron reduction) or reduced to hydrogen peroxide (two-electron reduction), the hydrogen peroxide is furtherly electrochemically reduced to water, or can be decomposed to O2, or desorbed into electrolyte [183]. ORR is a crucial reaction in many energy conversion devices such as low-temperature hydrogen and alcohol fuel cells, microbial fuel cells, and metal-air batteries [184]. The abundance of renewable and sustainable biomass on earth can serve as cost-effective carbon precursors to produce activated porous carbon with high SSA, well-ordered porosity and adjusted pore size, which could be used as a good alternative to transition metal-based electrocatalysts. Thus, the use of earth-rich biomass to produce carbon has great potential application in the field of ORR.

For example, Sekhon et al. synthesized coconut shell-derived porous carbon based on pyrolysis and activation to generate porous carbon (Fig. 21 a) [185]. Because of the multi-tubular hollow structure, coconut shell fiber can be regarded as a good starting material for the synthesis of 3D layered porous carbon. The obtained activated carbon (AC) possessed high SSA (1194 m2 g−1) and overall pore volume (0.528 cm3 g−1). Its pore size distribution of carbon allows positively charged carbon atoms to function as active sites to absorb oxygen. Li et al. synthesized N-doped porous carbon from plum blossom and melamine via KOH activation (Fig. 21b) [186]. The unique honeycomb structure and ultrahigh SSA (2118.9 m2 g−1) and multiple nitrogen coordination in the carbon matrix offers a high catalytic activity (E1/2 = 0.82 V and JL = 5.96 mA cm−2), predominant long-term stability (current retention of 91.2% after 50000 s) and good methanol resistance, which is comparable to commercial Pt/C.

Porosity is a vital factor that affects ORR since microporous structure can produce more reaction sites together with accelerated mass transfer. Kim et al. fabricated N, P co-doped porous pine pollen carbon (PPC) carbon through carbonization of pine pollen (Fig. 22 a) [187]. The large surface area (465 m2 g−1) and micropore (0.6–2.1 nm) can accommodate more active sites and effectively improve the performance of ORR. This idea was also confirmed by the choice of Melaleuca L (MLBs) to synthesize B, N-doped porous carbon (CBNC) for ORR by He et al. (Fig. 22b) [188]. The CBNC has a distinctive porous texture, a large SSA (1041 m2 g−1) and pore size range of 0.5–5 nm and homogeneous distribution of Co, B and N atoms. The superior overall ORR performance (jk = 8.2 mA cm−2, E1/2 = 0.82 V) suggests densely exposed active sites and a shortened mass transfer pathway which favors ORR.

Converting nitrogen (N2) into ammonia (NH3) under mild reaction condition have been regarded as the most promising chemical reaction. Electrochemical nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR) have come to the stage because it meets the requirements of activation and cleaving of the inert NN bonds [189,190]. The NRR process follows a mechanism of association and dissociation way. During the association pathway, adsorption and protonation of N2 molecules takes place in the distal or alternative routes to form NH3. In the dissociative mechanisms, NN triple bond breakage, N atom adsorption and protonation steps occurs [191,192].

There is an urgent need to seek various active and selective promising electrocatalyst because of the demanded activation of N–N bonds. The state of art and most effective electrocatalyst for NRR are precious metal like Ag and transition metal compounds. However, the poor selectivity and poor ability of nitrogen molecular absorption would greatly affect the yield of NRR and its practical application. Thus, biomass-based metal-free electrocatalyst have gained much attention owing to its low price, high surface area and excellent electrochemical properties [193]. For instance, Zeng et al. prepared pine tower-based metal-free porous carbon electrocatalyst (PT-KOH) via carbonization and KOH activation method (Fig. 23 a) [194]. The obtained PT-KOH possessed high efficiency and selectivity to convert N2 to NH3 with a considerable NH3 yield of 16.15 μg h−1 mg−1 cat. and a faradaic efficiency (FE) of 3.69% under −0.6 V vs. RHE in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. Meanwhile, kapok fiber derived O doped hollow carbon microtubes (O-KFCNTs) with excellent selectivity and electrochemical stability were synthesized via carbonization process [195]. The O-KFCNTs possessed high FE of 9.1% at −0.80 V vs. RHE and NH3 yield rate of 25.12 μg h−1 mg−1 cat. at −0.85 vs. RHE.

The hierarchically porous structure may contribute to the high electrocatalytic activity that facilitate the mass transfer. Yang et al. fabricated nitrogen rich carbon material (NFC) with micro-and mesopores derived from cicada sloughs through annealing and activation process (Fig. 23b) [196]. As shown in Fig. 23c, the obtained NFC-900 with high SSA promoted the conversion of N2 to NH3, which dramatically enhanced the NH3 yield (15.7 μg h−1 mg−1 cat.). Furthermore, the N2 reduction rate is enhanced with the increasing catalytic temperatures. Under high temperature, the mass transfer rate of reactants is faster than that at low temperature. Meanwhile, Zhao et al. fabricated alfalfa-derived N doped carbon (NPC) with porous structure through a pyrolysis process [197]. The sample pyrolyzed at 500 °C showed outstanding NRR activity with a NH3 yield of 1.31 mmol h−1 g−1 cat. (22.3 mg h−1 g−1 cat.) and a FE of 9.98% at −0.4 V (vs RHE) in 0.005 M H2SO4 solution. These outcomes can be attributed to the formation of more catalytically active sites on the catalyst surface due to the incorporation of pyridine-N into NPC-500, thus facilitates the formation of NH3 during NRR.

Besides CO2 capture and storage, converting CO2 into high valued fuels can reduce the concentration of CO2 in the environment and produce fuels to relieve the fossil fuel shortage [198,199]. The electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) had been considered as an ideal technology owing to its mild reaction condition, easy manipulation [200]. However, electrochemical CO2RR suffers from high intrinsic energy barriers, low selectivity, inability to retain high catalytic activity over a long duration, and unavoidable competing HER, thus, electrocatalyst with high efficiency and selectivity is a key point to achieve rapid reaction kinetics by overcoming the intrinsic barriers and achieving rapid reaction kinetics. Biomass based carbon materials for CO2RR have gained much attention because of its low price, high SSA, controllable electrochemical activity, and good conductivity [201,202].

N-doped metal-free carbon is considered as the most promising candidate to the metal based CO2RR catalysts, since doped N not only show high electronegativity which can efficiently tune the electronic structure of carbon skeleton, but also prompt transport of reactants and electrolytes, which is conductive to achieve highly efficient CO2 electrocatalysis [203,204]. For instance, Hao et al. synthesized cedar biomass-based N doped graphitized carbon (CB-NGC) for CO2RR via pyrolysis process under melamine and FeCl3 activator (Fig. 24 a) [205]. The obtained CB-NGC possessed high content of N (5.38%), high SSA (1673.6 m2 g−1), rich nanopores as well as enough active N sites, therefore contribute to a high FE of 91% at 0.56 V (vs. RHE) and minimum 20 h long-term stability. Meanwhile, Typha based N doped nanoporous carbon sheets (NC) through hydrothermal and KOH activation method was also reported (Fig. 24b) [206]. The NC carbonized at 900 °C exhibited outstanding catalytic performance with low onset potential (−0.3 V), high stability for 10 h, and high FE of CO about 90%. Its dense large pore structure and micropores (<1 nm) may promote the transportation and capture of CO2, thus increasing the concentration of CO2 at the reaction interface. Moreover, the high pyridinic N can reduce the free-energy barriers of COOH*, resulting in high activity and selectivity. Deng et al. prepared nitrogen doped hierarchical carbon decorated by CDs by employing municipal sludge as precursor under pyrolysis process [207]. The obtained carbon exhibited a whole FE of 90.14% at −0.71 V vs. RHE and excellent stability during the whole electrocatalytic reaction cycles. Algae based porous carbonaceous material with high pyridinic N content was also constructed by Hu's team employing facial thermochemical approach, which possessed outstanding CO2RR properties with a maximum FE of CO of 97.6% under −0.55 V vs RHE (Fig. 24c) [208]. Fig. 24d shows that the E-NC-900 displayed a higher current density and FE than E-NC-700 and E-NC-1100. The moderate nitrogen content and surface area of the E-NC-900 material made it the most favorable material toward electrocatalytic CO2 compared to the E-NC-700 (high nitrogen content but low surface area) and E-NC-1100 (low nitrogen content but high surface area) materials.

The lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) worked by moving Li ions between the positive and negative electrodes. The typical units of LIBs include a separator immersed in the electrolyte and two electrodes [209,210]. They are widely used in energy storage systems such as mobile phones, computers or automobiles primarily due to their high energy density, superior cycling performance as well as low self-discharge rates [211]. For LIBs, the anode material is generally graphite and biomass-based carbon. However, the low specific capacity of graphite in theory hinders the realization of fast charging [212]. Therefore, the biomass-based carbon is a suitable choice for the manufacture of rapid charging anodes. This is because biomass-based materials with a high surface area and unique structural characteristics (e.g., versatile pore structure, abundant surface functional groups, and various inorganic species) could enhance the diffusion of Li ions and provide a large interface of the electrode/electrolyte, thus favoring the electrochemical reactions in LIBs. Moreover, the specific capacity of most of biomass-based materials is much higher than that of conventional graphite materials [213].

Herein, by selecting buckwheat hulls and CaCl2 as precursor and activator, Yu et al. propose a protocol to make porous carbon anodes for sustained LIBs (Fig. 25 a) [214]. After carbonization activation, the generated buckwheat hull-based (BHC) carbon with porous structure can facilitate the charge transfer by shortening the transfer distance and provide a larger lithium electrode/electrolyte interface that contribute to the specific capacity of 715.0 mA h g−1 after 150 cycles at 0.2C with excellent cycling performance and cycling stability. The porous carbon extracted from jute fibers was treated by copper chloride activation method which was then employed as the anode for LIBs (Fig. 25b) [113]. The abundant porous structure with large BET surface areas (2043.5 m2 g−1) could supply numerous active sites and improve the rate performance, as a consequence, excellent specific charge capacity up to 580.4 mA h g−1 at 0.2C was achieved after 100 cycles.

Silicon is widely employed in LIBs as an anode material with ultra-high theoretical capacity with relatively low cycling stability. In this regard, a strategy of directly convert the biomass into a silicon/carbon composite structural anode was established to counter the low cycling stability of silicon anode. Such a biomass carbon matrix greatly improves its electronic conductivity by closely surrounding silicon nanoparticles and dramatically reduced the absolute stress/strain of the material in multiple lithiation-delithiation procedures [215]. For example, Ma et al. design an anode material, silicon complexed with biomass waste (rice husk) derived carbon, which enabled a superior electrochemical stability (Fig. 25c) [216]. It delivers a capacity retention rate of 90% after 150 cycles at 0.5C and a high charge capacity of 420.7 mA h g−1at 3C. He et al. also reported a LIBs anode material (PCF/Si) with porous carbon framework (PCF) consisting of elongated internal through-holes and a large number of micropores in the carbon walls by pretreatment grape stem (Fig. 25d) [217]. Since the pore effectively reduces the decomposition of the electrolyte, PCF/Si composites showed good discharge capacity (1006 mA h g−1 at 0.2 A g−1) together with strong cycling stability (891 mA h g−1 retained after 400 cycles). Based on studies mentioned above, biomass-derived silicon/carbon composite anode materials provide a new idea for the optimization of high energy density batteries.

Meanwhile, N-doping can confer biomass-derived 3D porous carbon with high SSA and rich porous structures, providing enhanced active sites and improved interaction with lithium ions [218]. Zhang et al. used alfalfa leaves as a nitrogen-containing carbon precursor to prepare the N-rich ordered mesoporous carbon (ALNOMC) with high SSA of 727.8 m2 g−1 (Fig. 26 a) [219]. When applied in LIBs, ALNOMC possessed an excellent reversible capacity of 1635 mA h g −1 after 10 cycles under 0.1 A g−1. Zeng et al. explored hierarchically NPC from stem medulla of water hyacinths with a specific area up to 1005 m2 g−1 which was then used as anode material of LIBs [220]. The honeycomb structure consisting of graphene-like sheets together with N dopant are the primary reason for the enhanced properties. Yan et al. synthesized a layered porous N, O co-doped carbon material (HPHCs) with opened 3D nanostructures using biomass soymilk as a precursor (Fig. 26b) [221]. The target products are capable of offering steady cycling performance (710 mA h g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 300 cycles for HPHC-a, and 610 mA h g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 200 cycles for HPHC-b) in LIBs as anodes materials (Fig. 26c). The micropores and mesopores of these N-doped biomass-based materials can function as transport channels for ions, shortening migration distances and thus achieving high-rate performance.

Furthermore, N, Si co-doped 3D porous structure also showed significant cycling properties and high-rate capability. For instance, Zhang et al. prepared bamboo-based Si@N/C composite (BC) [222]. The N-doped amorphous carbon can efficiently reduce the charge transfer resistance, while pristine silicon nanowire arrays enable 1D diffusion channel of Li ions, therefore a reversible capacity of 603 mA h g−1 after 120 cycles at 200 mA g−1 as well as high capacity of 360 mA h g−1 at 1.6 A g−1 can be obtained in LIBs.

Above findings support the hypothesis that enhancement of LIBs performance by the biomass-based distinctive hierarchical porous structure which exposes more active sites, facilitates the diffusion and transport of lithium ions, increases the electrical conductivity, and effectively compensates for its shortcomings when compounded with silicon, thus offering a promising future.

Lithium sulfur batteries (LSBs) operated by the electrochemical reactions between lithium metal anodes and sulfur-based cathodes. As a cathode material, the sulfur possesses the benefits of rich resources, cost effective, and eco-friendly. However, LSBs suffer from poor sulfur utilization and low long-term cycling stability, high expansion of volume as well as low Coulombic efficiency, which hinder its practical application. Carbon materials have large surface area, high conductivity and diversity of nanostructure, as a sulfur host, can improve the conductivity and alleviate the expansion effect by providing volume space. At the same time, biomass derived carbon with superior morphological structure can improve adsorption of polysulfides, reduce the dissolution of intermediate polysulfides, thereby improve the active material utilization and optimize the poor cycle performance [223,224]. Chang et al. used macadamia nut shells derived carbon and CNTs as the negative electrode material for LSBs [225]. The macadamia shell has a compact structure which delivers abundance of active sites in electrochemical reaction. Furthermore, the CNTs limit the ‘shuttle effect’ and provide path for charge transfer over long distance. All these factors result in original discharge specific capacity up to 1252.6 mA h g−1, and after 200 cycles it retains at 717.3 mA h g−1 under 0.2C.

The biomass derived carbon was employed as both cathode material and internal separator in LSBs. Wang et al. realized W/Mo compound-modified CNFs derived from bayberry tannin with rich functional groups (-NH2, –COOH, etc.) through single-step calcination. When used as a sulfur host and functional separators, the rate performance was as high as 552.75 mA h g−1 (10C) with a retention of 91.45% after 500 cycles at 2C (Fig. 27 a) [226]. Accordingly, Fe/Fe3C-filled multi-walled nanotubes (Fe/Fe3C-MWCNTs) were prepared from an activated carbon textile (ACTs). Cotton woven fabrics were used as a substrate and carbon source which can effectively buffer sulfur volume fluctuations and trap polysulfide due to their excellent mechanical properties [227]. Thus, the Fe/Fe3CMWCNT@ACT/S composite was assembled as a cathode for high-performance LSBs, which showed superior capacity retention of 95.5% after 400 cycles as well as excellent lifetime of 1000 cycles at 1.0C.

Preparing heteroatom doped carbon is a commonly strategy to optimize poor electrochemical properties caused by polysulfide shuttles. For instance, Fan et al. fabricated N, O co-doped tube-like porous carbon from fluffy catkins for LSBs (Fig. 27b) [228]. Tubular carbon structures with high sulfur content of 82.5 wt%, interconnected frames, and unique mesopores could facilitate ion diffusion, results in high discharge capacity with 540 mA h g−1 and capacity retention with 77.5% after 500 cycles under 0.5C.

Meanwhile, biomass can be converted into interlayer structure with distinctive electrochemical properties [229] or used as the surface modifier for commercial glass fiber (GF) membrane [230]. It enhances the sulfur capacity and regulates the dissolution and diffusion process of polysulfide, restrains the establishment of lithium dendrite. Additionally, the introduction of transition metal can also achieve optimized surface structure of biomass based carbon to improve the electrochemical performances of LSBs. Zhang et al. used carbon salt (CaCO3 and K2CO3) to pyrolysis alfalfa porous carbon to obtain high surface area and metal distributed material M-BPC (M = Ni, Co, Fe, Cu, and Mn) (Fig. 27c) [231]. Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of the LSBs at different current densities are shown in Fig. 27d. At large current density of 2C, the Ni-BPC-modified battery still maintained a distinct discharge plateau and a high discharge capacity with smaller polarization. The as-synthesized Ni-BPC possesses a well-developed porous structure and enables the adsorption of lithium polysulfide and exposure of active site. The diatomic Ni2 center enhances the diffusion rate of Li ions, adjusts the homogeneous deposition of discharge products, and increases active sites. In summary, biomass derived carbon can improve the electrical conductivity of composites, supply the volume space and reduce the dissolution of polysulfides.

Lithium oxygen batteries (LOBs) is composed of metal Li as anode and porous carbon as cathode. The cathode discharge reaction involves ORR to form alkali oxides while the charging reaction involves OER to decompose the discharge product. Since the charging and discharging process involves the diffusion of oxygen, the cathode requires a suitable porosity to provide channels for the oxygen to enter and exit [232]. Accordingly, biomass-derived porous carbon have been widely regarded as promising cathode materials for LOBs due to high surface area, corrosion resistance, electrical conductivity and excellent ORR/OER activity. The heteroatom doping could provide more active sites and high electronic conductivity and boost electrochemical performance, and therefore exhibits especially outstanding ORR properties [233]. Jiang et al. prepared P doped pinecone-based carbon (PPHC) with distinctive structure, active P sites, high surface area, and large number of surface defects. The unique honeycomb-like structure promotes a synergistic effect of O2 and Li + diffusion [234].

Besides, constructing various structure is another approach to adjust the performance of carbon. 3D hierarchically porous carbon supply a large number of reaction sites, accessible paths to charge/mass transfer, therefore promote oxygen diffusion (Fig. 28 a) [235]. Being a new type of cathode nanostructure in LOBs, multiple cross-linked channels in 3D carbon provide adequate space for the storage of insoluble discharge products. Betel nut based layered porous carbon for LOBs were synthesized by Arjunan's group (Fig. 28b) [236]. Its well-ordered and merged tubular morphology with SSA of 768 m2 g−1 provides sufficient path for diffusion, resulting in a discharge capacity of 9560 mA h g−1 at 100 mA g−1. Sun’ group also engineered a Typha leaf based carbon (PTCT) for bio-enhanced cathode by top-down method from phloem tissue (Fig. 28c) [237]. Fig. 28d reveals that even after 270 cycles, the terminal discharge voltage was still above 2.5 V for LOBs with the PTCT cathode, showing good cycling stability. The microchannels with layered porous walls of the Typha leaf maximize the expose of active sites and ensure the mechanical strength of intact framework during the cycling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potassium and sodium ion batteries (KIBs and SIBs)
ce:section.ce:para: A biomass derived porous carbon matrix can concurrently facilitate the diffusion of electrolyte solutions, optimize kinetics of electrode reaction, and improve the electrical conductivity for rapid electron and ion transport. Porous carbon materials with high N content were prepared from buckwheat and CaCO3 as template via pyrolysis [238]. The interconnected porous structure gives a high initial reversible capacity of 258 mA h g−1 and good cycle capability of 185 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles [239]. Chen et al. used seafood waste derivates-chitin for the preparation of N-doped hierarchically porous carbon microsphere (NCS) which possessed surface driven potassium storage (Fig. 29 a) [240]. At 72C, NCS electrode performed dramatic rate capability of 154 mA h g−1 and long cycle life of 4000 cycles (Fig. 29b). The layered porous microstructure and N dopant shows stronger potassium adsorption capacity and improved electron/ion conductivity which contribute to the KIBs. Regarding SIB, the N, P co-doped hard carbon prepared from tea also provides high reversible capacity (326.1 mA h g−1 at 28 mA g−1), high initial coulombic efficiency (ICE = 90% at 28 mA g−1), and superior rate performance (224.5 mA h g−1 at 1400 mA g−1) [241].

Supercapacitors (SCs) are considered as a promising energy storage device and have atterrated much attention due to its rapid charge and discharge, high stability, wide working temperature range as well as energy saving property compared to other energy storage devices. Among which, electrode material is a crucial factor that can influence the properties of supercapacitor, electrolytes and diaphragms [242–245]. However, efforts are still required to develop desired electrode material with high energy density, high rate performance [246]. At present, sustainable carbon materials especially those derived from biomass with controllable porous structure, high SSA and conductivity has greater potential as the electrode material for SCs [247,248]. Carbon materials with localized graphitic structure can not only improve the electrical conductivity, but also reduce the internal resistance and stabilize the structure. In addition, appropriate heteroatom doping can introduce functional group, improve the surface wettability of electrode, and provide additional faradaic pseudo-capacitance [249].

Biomass based porous carbon for supercapacitors should have a natural porosity, a highly graphitized and layered porous structure, which will provide more channels for charge transfer during electrochemical reaction [250]. For instance, Tan's group made full use of natural tubular structure of dandelion flower to prepare porous carbon by one-step activation and carbonization of K2FeO4 (Fig. 30 a) [153]. The final product has a higher degree of graphitization, larger SSA and exhibits an outstanding electrochemical property with specific capacitance of 309 F g−1 and energy density of 14.22 Wh kg−1. Orange peel based porous carbon by one-step carbonization method was also reported (Fig. 30b). The natural porous channel of orange peel with high porosity of 74.6% affords porous carbon with a high SSA of 860 m2 g−1. The as prepared supercapacitor from orange peel derived carbon reaches surface capacitance of 3987 mF cm−2 [251]. Cao et al. also take advantage of lignin and cellulose acetate to fabricated biomass-based CNFs with a specific capacitance of 346.6 F g−1 and a high-energy density of 31.5 Wh kg−1 under 400 W kg−1 via a novel phosphating, oxidation and post-carbonization approach [135]. Meanwhile, Meshal et al. applied waste tea as precursor under carbonization and KOH activation to prepare activated carbon to produce supercapacitors [252]. The as-synthesized device possessed an outstanding performance with specific energy of 25.4 Wh kg−1 under 425 W kg−1 and capacity retention of 90% after 3000 GCD series.

Micro-, meso- and macro-pore in 3D hierarchical porous carbon can improve the electrochemical properties via different pathways [246]. For instance, Sun et al. synthesized 3D interconnected porous carbon material by the self-assembly of biomass derivates under carbonization and activation method (Fig. 30c) [253]. The as-prepared material showed a worm-like microporous structure with uniform mesoporous structures and collapsed structures, exhibited high specific capacitance of 332 F g−1, and maintained capacitance retention of 97% as well as Coulombic efficiency of 100% over 10000 cycles.

Self-heteroatom doping of biomass based carbon can resulted in a higher pseudo capacitance and cycling stability of electrode materials [254]. Yang et al. recently utilized bamboo fungus to prepare N/O co-doped layered porous 3D carbon nano foam (ZCH-PC) [255]. The ZCH-PC exhibited a high specific capacitance of 367 F g−1. Besides, the assembled supercapacitor provides extraordinary energy density of 24.6 Wh kg−1 at power density of 400 W kg−1. Hu et al. also synthesized shrimp shell derived N, O co-doped hierarchical porous carbonaceous material via carbonization and KOH activation for constructing flexible supercapacitor system (Fig. 30d) [256]. The material showed micro- and meso-pores with a unique layered structure which can accelerate ion transport and enhance energy storage capacity. When served as electrode, it exhibited high specific capacitance (356.23 F g−1 at 0.1 A), energy density (26.71 Wh kg−1 under 250 W kg−1) and cycle stability (over 90% at 5000 charge/discharge cycles).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other energy conversion and storage system
