ce:section.ce:para: Biomass based carbon can be effectively used in energy conversion and storage devices in microbial fuel cells and microwave absorption due to high SSA and adjustable porous structure [257,258]. MFCs are used to treat wastewater and generate electricity. Lignocellulosic biomass (LCB) based MFCs offer an energy-producing system which is regarded as one of the sustainable energy sources [259]. Li et al. extracted cellulose out of coconut fiber by alkali boiling combined with purification in NaClO2 solution (Fig. 31 a) [260]. Among them, CON-900 shows a multi-stage pore structure with a high SSA of 652 m2 g−1 and abundant active sites, consisting of pyridine nitrogen, graphite nitrogen and oxygen-containing species. The excellent properties mentioned above contribute to mass transport and expose of active sites. When employed CON-900-MFC as air cathodes, maximum power density of 1014 ± 23 mW m−2 can be obtained.

Biomass-based porous carbon with nano-or micropores are considered as effective materials for microwave absorption [261]. The porous structure of biomass carbon enables a superior absorption performance which can well overcome the disadvantages of poor impedance matching, weak absorption, high loading of filler and poor stability of traditional microwave absorbers [262–264]. Liu et al. successfully fabricated dried apple derived porous C/SiC composite (HPCC) (Fig. 31b) [265]. After freeze-drying, the apple's natural pore structure can be fully retained at a diameter of 50–100 μm where the micropores may act as dihedral angles of microwave reflection. Furthermore, carbonized apple-derived porous material increases microwave reflection and scattering time along with the spread path. The layered structure is beneficial to improve the microwave absorption properties of HPCC. The Stamen-like internal morphology and bird's nest-like external morphology of HPCC could act as a transition structure to reduce the reflectivity of incident microwaves. At 1.5 mm thickness, HPCC has a reflection loss of −44.2 dB and a wide effective absorption bandwidth of 4.2 GHz.

As is reviewed here, biomass-derived carbon is widely employed in electrocatalysis, energy conversion and storage. Because of high surface area, naturally abundant heteroatoms, and good electronic conductivity, it serves as supports for active constituents for an efficient and sustainable electrocatalyst. Moreover, the biomass derived carbon materials exhibit an optimized pore structure for shortening ion diffusion pathways and favorable surface properties for avoiding large irreversible capacities. When employed in secondary batteries, the lithium/sodium/potassium ions are inserted into the carbon framework, enhanced the specific capacity and rate performance can be expected. In SCs, the charge transfer mechanism strongly depends on the surface area accessible to electrolytic ions. The pore size and shape, surface functionality and electrical conductivity of biomass based carbon play a key role in increasing the specific capacitance. Among these factors, pore size has a significant contribution to determine the specific capacitance. The nature of the pore size distribution, more importantly, the presence of both micropores and mesopores, rules the penetration or mobility of ions in the porous structure.

In this review, we present the recent advances of the biomass-derived carbon with particular focus on the general synthetic strategy, various dimensions of carbon structures and advanced application in electrocatalysis, secondary batteries, supercapacitors and other energy storage devices. The conversion of biomass into various architecture carbon starts with the conventional pyrolysis and hydrothermal carbonization and evolved into chemical vapor deposition, molten salt carbonization, template method and other methods. Among these synthetic approaches, temperature plays an important role in the transformation process of graphitization along with the suitable activators to tune textural properties. In addition, the different component and morphology of biomass sources under different synthetic method could regulate different dimensional carbon nanomaterials with different physico-chemical properties. Then, diverse morphology of biomass-derived carbon was comprehensively summarized from spherical (0D), fibrous and tubular (1D), sheet-like (2D) to hierarchical skeleton structure (3D). These architecture displays multiple pores, high surface area, tunable structure, excellent conductivity, enables highly activity in electrocatalysis including HER, OER, ORR, NRR, CO2RR. Due to favorable ion and electron transport channel, hierarchical structure with expose of active sites, surface functional groups, the biomass-derived multi-dimensional carbon acts as prospective negative electrode materials can promote ion electron transfer in sustainable batteries (LIBs, LSBs, LOBs, SIBs, KIBs), supercapacitors, micro-fuel cells and microwave absorption.

Although significant progress in recent years has been made in biomass-based carbon nanostructure for various electrocatalysis, energy storage and conversion, there are still many challenges. Firstly, since huge amount of natural biomass was produced and not fully utilized. The current application of diverse biomass derived carbon nanostructure mainly focused on the laboratory production. Thus, strengthening the large-scale synthetic strategy should be developed to transfer them directly into electrode materials or substrates in a facile and ecofriendly manner to achieve sustainable development strategies. Secondly, the crude biomass with complex composition and morphology can yield a well-behaved porous structure and surface chemistry. However, it is difficult to interpret the underlying mechanism, especially for the porosity and heteroatom doping, the exact type of heteroatom species in the carbon matrix is ambiguous. Therefore, screening out suitable biomass sources are necessary to precise control the physicochemical property. Besides, the comprehensive detailed in situ characterization is highly desirable to well illustrate the relationship between electrochemical performance and biomass derived carbon materials.
